questions.q: Identify the two specific mistakes LinkedIn made in how it stored its users' passwords.
options: It used a fast hash function (SHA-1), and its hashes were unsalted
It stored passwords as plaintext, and it failed to use HTTPS on the login page
It encrypted passwords with a weak key, and it reused the same key for all users
It used a slow hash function, and it stored the salt separately from the database
questions.explanation: The script is explicit that LinkedIn made two mistakes: it chose SHA-1, a fast, general-purpose hash, and its hashes were unsalted. It is a common trap to assume the passwords were stored as plaintext — the script stresses they were NOT, LinkedIn was hashing, just doing it badly. Note that passwords are hashed, not encrypted, so the encryption distractor describes the wrong technique entirely.

questions.q: Outline what a hash function does when a website stores a password securely.
options: It encrypts the password so it can be decrypted later with a key
It is a one-way scrambler that turns the password into a fixed-length fingerprint that cannot be reversed back into the password
It compresses the password into a smaller string to save database space
It splits the password into pieces stored across several servers
questions.explanation: The script describes a hash function as a 'one-way scrambler' that produces a fixed-length fingerprint with 'no reverse gear' — you can go from password to fingerprint but not back. The most tempting wrong answer is encryption (A), but encryption is two-way and reversible with a key, which is exactly what you do NOT want for a password. The site stores only the fingerprint and re-hashes login attempts to compare.

questions.q: Explain why a deliberately slow hash function is a security advantage for storing passwords.
options: A slow hash uses less server memory, leaving more resources for other tasks
A slow hash makes legitimate logins take longer, discouraging users from logging in too often
Because attackers crack hashes by guessing passwords forwards, a slow hash limits them to far fewer guesses per second, making brute-forcing the list economically hopeless
A slow hash automatically adds a salt to each password as it runs
questions.explanation: Since a hash can't be reversed, attackers recover passwords by guessing candidates, hashing them forwards, and checking for matches. A fast hash like SHA-1 lets them try billions of guesses per second; a slow hash makes each guess expensive, so the same attack would take centuries. The script's line is 'Slow is a feature.' Option D confuses slowness with salting — they are two separate defences.

questions.q: Describe what a salt is and the job it performs in password storage.
options: A small chunk of random data, unique per user, mixed into the password before hashing so that identical passwords produce different fingerprints
A shared secret key, identical for all users, that encrypts the password before storage
A checksum added after hashing to detect whether the stored fingerprint has been tampered with
A second password the user must enter to confirm their identity
questions.explanation: The script defines a salt as 'a little chunk of random data — different for every single user — that you mix into the password before you hash it.' Its job is to ensure two users with the same password get different fingerprints. The shared-key distractor is wrong because a salt is unique per user (not shared) and is not a key, and salting is not encryption.

questions.q: Explain how a unique salt defeats a precomputed lookup table attack.
options: It encrypts the lookup table so the attacker cannot read it
It slows down the hash function so the table takes too long to build
It hides the fingerprints in a separate database the attacker cannot reach
It makes each user's fingerprint depend on their unique salt, so a table built from plain dictionary words never matches and the attacker must crack each account separately
questions.explanation: A precomputed table maps common passwords to their fingerprints so an attacker can look up stolen hashes instantly. Because each user's salt is mixed in before hashing, none of the table's pre-built fingerprints will match, forcing the attacker to redo all the work for every single user. The script notes salt doesn't make one password uncrackable, but it makes the 'bulk, wholesale, crack-them-all-at-once approach impossible.' The 'slows down the hash' distractor describes a separate defence, not what defeats the table.

questions.q: Distinguish between hashing and encryption in the context of storing passwords.
options: Hashing is faster than encryption, which is why hashing is preferred for passwords
Encryption is one-way and hashing is two-way, so encryption is the safer choice for passwords
They are the same process; 'hashing' and 'encryption' are just two names for scrambling data
Hashing is one-way with no key and no reverse gear, whereas encryption is two-way and designed to be decrypted with a key — so passwords must be hashed, never encrypted
questions.explanation: The script draws this distinction sharply: encryption is two-way and reversible with a key (good for a message you need to read later), while hashing is one-way with no key and no reverse gear. If passwords were encrypted, a stolen key or a careless insider could recover every password in plaintext. The exam rule to carry: 'you hash passwords, you don't encrypt them.' The 'hashing is faster' distractor is misleading — speed is not the reason; in fact, for passwords you want the hash to be slow.

questions.q: After cracking LinkedIn passwords, attackers tried the same email-and-password pairs on Gmail, PayPal and Amazon. Identify the name of this technique and why it works.
options: Phishing — it works because users are tricked into entering passwords on fake sites
Credential stuffing — it works because many people reuse the same password across multiple sites
A precomputed table attack — it works because the hashes were unsalted
A denial-of-service attack — it works by overwhelming the other sites with login attempts
questions.explanation: The script names this credential stuffing: attackers take a leaked username-and-password pair and try it 'on site after site after site,' betting the user reused it. It works because of password reuse, which 'turns one company's breach into your problem,' breaking into accounts on services that were never breached. Phishing (A) is a different attack involving deception, not automated reuse of already-stolen pairs.

questions.q: State the three rules for secure password storage that the episode draws out as its core examinable lesson.
options: Encrypt the password, store the key separately, and rotate the key monthly
Use a fast hash for speed, store passwords in plaintext backups, and limit login attempts
Use a one-way hash, give every password a unique salt, and use a deliberately slow, expensive function
Compress the password, add a shared salt, and verify with a checksum
questions.explanation: The script's three lessons are: passwords must be stored using a one-way hash (never reversible encryption); every password gets a unique per-user salt; and the hash must be slow and expensive on purpose. Get those three right and a stolen database is 'a minor embarrassment instead of a catastrophe.' The encryption distractor is wrong on the first principle — encryption is reversible, which is exactly the danger to avoid.

questions.q: A developer stores passwords using a fast hash but adds a unique salt per user. Explain why this is still insufficient.
options: The salt makes the system secure, so no further protection is needed
Salting defeats precomputed tables, but a fast hash still lets an attacker guess billions of candidates per second against each salted account, so weak passwords fall quickly
Adding a salt to a fast hash makes the database slower for legitimate users and should be removed
Salts can be reversed, so the attacker can simply remove the salt and use a precomputed table
questions.explanation: Salt and slowness are two separate, complementary defences. The salt forces the attacker to attack each account individually instead of all at once, but with a fast hash they can still run billions of guesses per second against each account, so common passwords are cracked almost instantly. This is precisely why the script lists all three requirements together — one-way hash, unique salt, AND a deliberately slow function. Option D is wrong because salts are not secret values that get 'reversed'; they work even when stored alongside the hash.

questions.q: The 2012 leak was first reported as 6.5 million accounts, but in 2016 the full scale surfaced. Identify the true number of credentials involved and the practical defence the episode says is yours to act on.
options: About 50 million credentials; the defence is to change your LinkedIn password every month
About 6.5 million credentials; the defence is to enable HTTPS on every website you visit
About 117 million credentials; the defence is to use a different password on every site, ideally with a password manager
About 200 million credentials; the defence is to rely on LinkedIn to salt its hashes in future
questions.explanation: The script states that in 2016 the full extent surfaced as around 117 million credentials — the 2012 figure of 6.5 million had been just the tip. The personal defence is using a different password everywhere, ideally with a password manager, so 'one site's bad day can never become the key to your whole digital life.' The HTTPS and 'rely on LinkedIn' distractors miss the point: the breach spread through password reuse, which is the user's responsibility to defend against, not something HTTPS or LinkedIn's own fixes can solve for you.
